title: Therapeutic Diets: Matching Nutrition to the Condition
subtitle: Across an assignment the nurse matches the right therapeutic diet to each patient — clear vs full liquid, texture-modified for dysphagia, low-sodium, renal, carbohydrate-consistent, and low-residue — and advances diets safely.

patient.name: Therapeutic Diets
patient.admitDate: The nurse matches each patient to the correct therapeutic diet and advances diets safely as tolerated.

nursesNote: The nurse matches each patient to the correct therapeutic diet. A clear liquid diet (broth, apple juice, plain gelatin, tea, clear ice pops) is see-through and used short-term around procedures; a full liquid diet adds milk, pudding, and cream soups as a transition. A mechanical soft or pureed diet with thickened liquids reduces aspiration risk in dysphagia. A low-sodium diet (~2 g/day) is used for heart failure, hypertension, and cirrhosis with ascites; a renal diet limits potassium and phosphorus in CKD/ESRD; a carbohydrate-consistent diet supports glucose control in diabetes; a low-residue (low-fiber) diet rests the bowel during an acute diverticulitis or IBD flare, while a high-fiber diet treats constipation and diverticulosis. NPO is maintained before surgery or with aspiration risk. Diets are advanced gradually (clear -> full -> soft -> regular) after bowel function returns and per order.
providerOrders: Match the therapeutic diet to each patient's condition
Use texture-modified foods and thickened liquids for dysphagia
Provide low-sodium for HF/HTN and a renal diet (low K/phosphorus) for CKD
Keep NPO patients without food or fluids before surgery or with aspiration risk
Advance diet gradually (clear -> full -> soft -> regular) as tolerated and ordered

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about therapeutic diets?

opts.text: A clear liquid diet includes broth, apple juice, and plain gelatin
A full liquid diet adds milk, pudding, and cream soups
A mechanical soft or pureed diet helps patients with swallowing difficulty
A clear liquid diet provides complete long-term nutrition
NPO means no food or fluids by mouth
Ice cream and milk are allowed on a clear liquid diet
A low-sodium diet is used for heart failure and hypertension
A carbohydrate-consistent diet supports glucose control in diabetes
A low-residue (low-fiber) diet is used during an acute diverticulitis flare
A high-potassium diet is recommended for end-stage renal disease
Thickened liquids reduce aspiration risk in dysphagia
A high-fiber diet is best during an acute bowel inflammation flare

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order advancing the diet after bowel surgery

opts.text: Confirm bowel function (flatus/sounds) and the provider order
Begin with clear liquids as tolerated
Advance to full liquids
Progress to a low-residue or soft diet
Resume a regular diet as tolerated

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Allowed on a CLEAR LIQUID diet?

opts.text: Apple juice without pulp
Beef broth
Milk
Plain gelatin
Cream of mushroom soup
Black coffee or tea
Orange juice with pulp
Ice pop without fruit pieces

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Setting up a dysphagia patient to eat safely
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Verify the ordered diet texture and liquid consistency
Position the patient upright at 90 degrees
Ensure suction is available and provide the modified meal
Encourage small bites, a slow pace, and chin-tuck
Keep upright 30-60 min after and document intake
Giving a low-sodium diet to a patient with heart failure
Serving whole nuts and raw vegetables during acute diverticulitis
Providing thickened liquids to a patient with dysphagia
Offering a banana and orange juice daily to a low-K renal patient
Advancing from clear to full liquids as tolerated post-op
Keeping a patient flat while eating with aspiration risk
Providing a carbohydrate-consistent diet for a diabetic patient
Giving solid food to a patient who is NPO for surgery
